era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the Ming Dynasty's consolidation under Emperor Yongle, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia, and the Great Schism in the Catholic Church. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War continues between England and France under a fragile truce. The Portuguese Age of Exploration is beginning under Prince Henry the Navigator. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty is at the height of its naval and diplomatic power, and Zheng He's treasure voyages are beginning.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse European records; Chinese sources may be underrepresented)
Indigenous Americas (reliance on post-conquest accounts and archaeology)

events.title: Zheng He's first treasure voyage departs from Nanjing
events.description: Admiral Zheng He (鄭和), commanding a massive treasure fleet of over 300 ships and 27,000 men, departs from Nanjing in early 1405 on the first of his seven great voyages. The fleet carries diplomatic gifts, trade goods, and military personnel, sailing to Southeast Asia, the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Arabian Peninsula. These voyages represent the peak of Ming naval power and exploration, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Chinese maritime supremacy. The voyages are sponsored by Emperor Yongle and reflect the Ming Dynasty's confidence and expansionist ambitions. The fleet's size and organization are unprecedented in world history at this time, far exceeding contemporary European naval capabilities.
key_figures: Tributary rulers from Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, Longjiang Baochuan (later compilation, 16th century)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's first voyage is well-documented in Ming imperial records. The departure date of early 1405 is firmly established in contemporary sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control over Yunnan and Annam
events.description: Emperor Yongle of the Ming Dynasty launches major military campaigns to consolidate control over Yunnan province in southwestern China and Annam (northern Vietnam). These campaigns are part of a broader effort to expand Ming territorial control and secure the southern and southwestern frontiers. The consolidation of these regions provides the Ming Dynasty with additional resources and manpower, enabling the mobilization of forces for Zheng He's treasure voyages and other imperial projects. However, the occupation of Annam is unpopular and will eventually lead to resistance movements and the restoration of Vietnamese independence.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Ming territorial expansion

events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation of Yunnan and Annam is documented in imperial records, but the specific dates are approximate. The campaigns occurred over several years, with major operations in 1405–1427.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming territorial expansion (1402–1424)
Enables resources for Zheng He's voyages (1405–1433)
Leads to Vietnamese resistance and independence (1427)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast begins under Prince Henry
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese expeditions begin systematic exploration of the West African coast. The first major expedition, led by João Gonçalves Zarco and Tristão Vaz Teixeira, reaches the Madeira Islands in 1405. These early voyages lay the groundwork for the Portuguese Age of Exploration and the eventual rounding of the Cape of Good Hope. The expeditions are motivated by economic gain (seeking gold and spices), religious conversion, and geographic curiosity. The Portuguese establish trading posts and forts along the African coast, initiating the transatlantic slave trade.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (15th-century compilation)
Portuguese royal records and trading post documentation

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented, but the exact date of the first expedition is somewhat uncertain. The discovery of the Madeira Islands is traditionally dated to 1405, but some sources place it in 1418 or later. The general trajectory of Portuguese exploration is confirmed; individual voyage dates may be approximate.
cross_references: Begins the Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)
Leads to the transatlantic slave trade (15th–19th centuries)

events.title: English-French truce continues under the Treaty of Paris
events.description: The Treaty of Paris (1396) continues to hold, maintaining a fragile truce between England and France in the Hundred Years' War. However, tensions are rising, particularly in France, where factional conflict between Burgundians and Armagnacs is beginning to emerge. The truce will eventually be broken by English military intervention under Henry V, leading to the Battle of Agincourt (1415) and the Treaty of Troyes (1420). In 1405, the situation remains relatively stable, but the underlying tensions are building.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Treaty of Paris truce is documented in royal records. The year 1405 is confirmed as a period of relative stability in English-French relations.
cross_references: Leads to 1407-001 (assassination of Louis of Orléans)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Portuguese discovery of the Madeira Islands is disputed. While traditionally dated to 1405, some modern scholarship places the discovery in 1418 or later. The significance of the 'discovery' is also contested: the islands may have been known to earlier navigators, and the Portuguese 'discovery' represents European colonization rather than the first human contact. The 'Age of Exploration' framing applied to Portuguese activities in 1405 is somewhat anachronistic; these were primarily trading and raiding expeditions, not systematic exploration in the later sense. The significance of Zheng He's voyages has been subject to historiographic debate: some scholars argue they were primarily diplomatic and tributary missions rather than voyages of discovery or trade expansion.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1405 is moderate but geographically uneven. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) provide detailed records of Zheng He's voyages and Ming territorial consolidation, reflecting imperial court perspectives. European events (English-French truce, Portuguese exploration) are documented in royal records and later chronicles, with a Western institutional bias. Vietnamese sources are later compilations, introducing potential dating uncertainties. African and indigenous American history remains poorly documented for this year, relying on later accounts and archaeology. The historiographic tradition privileges European and East Asian sources, potentially marginalizing other regions. The 'Age of Exploration' narrative itself reflects a Eurocentric historiographic tradition that privileges European agency and discovery over non-European maritime activity (such as Zheng He's voyages, which were arguably more extensive but are often marginalized in Western historical accounts).

graph_edges.note: Departure of Zheng He's first voyage in 1405 led to its continuation through Southeast Asia in 1406
Ming consolidation of Annam in 1405 eventually led to Vietnamese resistance and independence in 1427
Portuguese exploration in 1405 is part of the broader Age of Exploration (15th–17th centuries)
English-French truce in 1405 was destabilized by the assassination of Louis of Orléans in 1407
